title: How Trump’s Venezuela raid is helping China's Taiwan battle plans

transcript.text: So, it seems likely that Beijing is
going to look closely at the US strike
on Venezuela. What lessons do you think
the leadership in Beijing will be hoping
to draw? I don't think they'll be
looking at this as a sort of template
for an attack on Taiwan because there
just too many differences in terms of
the terrain. But I do think they will
look very closely at different
components of it to help hone their own
abilities in particular to conduct a
decapitation strike on Taiwan's
leadership because you know we know
that's something they've talked openly
about for some time as one part of you
know a potential assault on Taiwan and
it seems to be something that they are
focusing on increasingly over the last
few years probably as a result of
Russia's failure to take out Ukraine's
president Vladimir Zalinski in the early
stages of the Russian invasion and then
his subsequent success in rallying both
domestic and international support for
for resistance against that invasion.
>> For someone who looks at the military
far less closely than you two, I think
the term decapitation strike is a bit
unsettling, I [laughter] think, to the
average person. Um but as we as we can
see the importance of this is clearly
increasing. How is the Chinese military
preparing for these decapitation
strikes?
>> So one of the ways is through physical
simulations in 2015. China's state
television uh showed footage of a a
similar mockup of the Taiwan
presidential palace somewhere in the
desert in northern China uh and they
were using that to practice an assault
on Taiwan's leadership. And that
training site has actually tripled in
size uh since 2020 according to some
satellite images that were published in
October by a Japanese think tank. And it
looks like um one reason for that
expansion is that they've added a bunch
of other ministries including a model of
the foreign ministry and the justice
ministry in downtown Taipei um as well
as that mockup of the presidential
palace.
>> I am so curious as to the level of
detail they get down to like when Taiwan
was um in office with they have their
pets take her pets taken into account.
It's kind of hard to wrap your head
around um just what level of detail
would be needed for that to be
effective. But when I was in Taipei, it
seemed like in the past few years,
there's been a lot more talk uh from the
military about decapitation style
strikes.
>> Yes, that's absolutely right. And
including in the most recent drills,
which just took place in December around
Taiwan, and they were called Justice
Mission 2025.
They involved, you know, dozens of
Chinese warships and military aircraft
and long range rockets being fired into
waters very close to Taiwan. And uh they
practiced a few things. Uh a blockade of
Taiwan's main ports. Um they simulated
operations to stop foreign forces from
intervening. But around the same time uh
the PLA senior colonel Jang Chur who is
uh at the National Defense University in
China, he appeared on state TV to talk
about the mission and he made it clear
that there was another element um of the
of operations that those exercises were
trying to practice for. Um he says the
exercise is uh simulating strikes
against symbolic target targets
associated with the ring leaders of
Taiwan independence. Um and this shows
that the PLA has formidable capabilities
to deliver precise sanctions against the
principal Taiwan independence criminals.
It's interesting that the colonel makes
that point so explicitly, especially in
light of China's threats in recent years
to impose criminal punishment, including
the death penalty against
pro-independence leaders in Taiwan.
>> That's right. Because China passed its
anti-session law back in 2005, I think,
but just in the last two years, you
know, it's really been stepping up those
threats to apply that law to people on
Taiwan. And of course, the US used
charges of narot terrorism to justify
grabbing Maduro.
>> So, it's not surprising why some people
are drawing parallels. Given what you
said about the PLA's relative lack of
experience, how well prepared is China
to pull off a decapitation operation of
the kind it's been practicing for?
Presumably they've made some progress
through all those simulations and other
drills they've been doing in recent
years. But I think there are still a
number of key obstacles and probably the
main one is that the people's liberation
army has yet to achieve the kind of high
level integration of space, air, land,
sea, cyber capabilities um that are
essential to pull off the kind of
complex operation that we saw in in
Karacus. There's been a big effort by
Xiinping um on over the last 10 years to
try and make those exercises more
focused on sort of real combat, but um I
think that's still, you know, a real
challenge for the PLA.
>> And it's not just the people and the
training, but also the kit. I've read a
lot about the failure of Venezuela's air
defense system, which used Chinese tech.
Um how concerned do you think China will
be about that? We still don't know
exactly what happened. Um, you know,
these radars could have failed because
of operator error. Uh, they could have
been sabotaged. They could have just
been poorly maintained. But I I think uh
their failure will be a big concern uh
for the Chinese government because you
know these are marketed as being able to
detect missiles and stealth aircraft
like America's F-22 fighter jet but in
this case uh they seem to have
completely failed. I think uh the
Chinese government will also be very
keen to analyze any information that
might have come from those radars before
they stopped operating in the weeks um
you know building up to this raid when
the Americans were conducting
um a lot of operations you know around
Venezuela's coast um and they'll be
interested to see you know right right
right right right right right right
right right right right right right
right right right right right right
right right right right right right
right right right right right right
right right right right right right
right right right right up until the
moment that the radars failed you know
what what if any data were they picking
up
>> so Jeremy we've covered the lessons that
Beijing and Taipei will be trying to
draw from the US strike on Venezuela.
But I wanted to ask, how do you think
the raid will actually impact their
actions going forward?
>> I think a lot depends on what happens in
the next few months really. Um, one
thing that China will be looking at very
closely is how their interests in
Venezuela are affected. Um, as well as
those in other parts of Latin America.
>> Right. There's a whole other episode we
could do on what the attack means for
China's interests in South America given
that Venezuela was the largest recipient
in the continent of official Chinese
loans and grants and that China in 2023
upgraded its relationship with Venezuela
to an all-weather partnership. Tana will
also be looking very closely at any
changes in the the balance of power
within the Trump administration between
different factions around Trump um and
whether the Venezuela raid strengthens
the so-called restrainers who want to
focus more on the American homeland at
the expense of China hawks who are more
committed to to Taiwan's defense. So
there are a lot of factors at play in
Beijing's calculations over Taiwan and
this strike is a new one but only part
of a much larger set of considerations.
>> The bottom line I think is Venezuela
itself is unlikely to change China's
calculations in the short term. It's
certainly a good case study for the PLA
as it tries to develop its various
capabilities, especially for its special
forces to be ready uh for an attack on
Taiwan. You know, we know Xiinping set
them goal of 2027 um to be capable of
achieving that. And then over the longer
term, if the Venezuela raid leads to
America getting bogged down there or in
other military escapades in the Western
Hemisphere or if China becomes concerned
uh about, you know, other autocracies
around the world being toppled or indeed
if America becomes overconfident about,
you know, its capacity to use force,
then, you know, it could it could become
one of several factors. s that start to
shape China's thinking on Taiwan.
[music]
